name: Embroker
funding_scale: $142M raised (Series C led by FTV Capital, June 2021)
distribution_notes: Direct-to-buyer digital platform (ONE platform) + Embroker Access broker portal for retail/wholesale brokers. MGA model with proprietary underwriting through Everspan and other carrier partners. Reinsured by Munich Re and Everest Re (A/A+ rated). Also building channel through billing automation partnership with Ascend.
primary_positioning: Digital-first insurance platform for startups and SMBs — 'radically easier business insurance'
key_differentiators: 10-minute online quote and bind
Bundled startup packages (D&O + EPLI + Tech E&O + Cyber)
ONE platform — single application for all lines
Claims of 25%+ premium savings vs. traditional
Proprietary underwriting via MGA model
AI Coverage Endorsement auto-included on every Tech E&O / Cyber quote (launched Aug 2025)
pricing_model: Generally lower premiums than Vouch, especially on bundled startup packages. Speed-to-quote is a key acquisition hook. MGA model gives them some underwriting flexibility on pricing.
strengths_vs_vouch: Lower premiums, especially for early-stage companies
Faster self-serve quote flow (10 min to quote and bind)
Broader vertical coverage (law firms, real estate, consulting that Vouch doesn't serve)
MGA model gives direct underwriting control
Embroker Access extends reach through broker channel
Longer track record (2015 vs. Vouch 2019)

AI Coverage Endorsement auto-included in Tech E&O / Cyber — addresses AI professional services, discrimination/bias, regulatory defense, and algorithm removal expenses (up to $150K) at full policy limits, no sublimits
weaknesses_vs_vouch: Shallow advisory relationship — self-serve model means gaps go unidentified
Limited carrier access compared to Vouch's 500+ carrier market
Poor customer service reputation (slow responses, refund issues per Trustpilot/G2)
No proactive coverage gap analysis or milestone-driven reviews
Self-serve COI and mid-term update processes are more manual
Policies often have restrictive terms (retroactive date resets, narrow exclusions) that aren't explained upfront
AI endorsement is self-serve — no advisor to assess whether limits, terms, and covered scenarios match a company's actual AI risk profile
where_we_win: Vouch wins when there's a triggering event — a fundraise, a major contract requirement, or a bad service experience — that forces the prospect to look beyond price. Coverage gap analysis is the closer. Vouch's advisory depth and full market access (broker model vs. Embroker's limited MGA paper) resonate once prospects realize what they're actually missing. IMPORTANT UPDATE (Aug 2025): Embroker now has an AI Coverage Endorsement auto-included in Tech E&O / Cyber. AI endorsements are no longer a unique Vouch differentiator vs. Embroker. Shift the AI angle to advisory depth — Vouch helps clients understand whether AI coverage terms actually match their specific risk profile; Embroker's self-serve model doesn't.
where_we_lose: Vouch loses or stalls when the prospect is purely cost-driven with no immediate coverage pressure. Embroker's self-serve speed and lower price win founders who just want to check the insurance box at company formation. 'Set it and forget it' inertia keeps existing Embroker clients from switching even when they should. Embroker's AI endorsement now also neutralizes a key Vouch differentiator in AI-vertical conversations.
objection_handling: 'Embroker is cheaper' → Run a coverage gap analysis. Show what the Embroker policy actually covers vs. what it appears to cover. Benchmark data demonstrates total cost of risk, not just premium.

'We already have Embroker' → Most Embroker clients bought a starter package and never revisited. Ask: when did you last review coverage? Do you know your retroactive dates? Have you raised funding since buying?

'Hard to compare policies' → Offer a transparent coverage walkthrough — Vouch advisors do side-by-side on policy terms, exclusions, and limits. This is a selling point: Embroker's self-serve model doesn't offer this.

'Embroker also has AI coverage now' → Embroker added an AI endorsement in Aug 2025 — that's a real product. The question is whether an endorsement you picked from a dropdown actually maps to your specific AI risk profile. Vouch advisors assess what you're building, how you're deploying AI, what your exposure actually is — and make sure coverage terms match. A self-serve endorsement doesn't do that work for you.
content_handling: Don't name. Lead with Vouch's advisory depth, coverage gap analysis capability, and milestone-driven scaling. Counter the 'cheap and fast' frame by emphasizing total cost of risk and hidden gaps in self-serve startup packages. For AI vertical content: do NOT lead with 'we have AI-specific endorsements' as a point of differentiation vs. Embroker — they now have one too. Instead, lead with advisory depth: understanding whether your AI coverage actually fits your risk requires expert guidance, not a checkbox.
gaps: Specific policy forms and exclusion language for the AI endorsement (retroactive date practices, exclusion carve-outs)
Revenue or premium volume figures
Whether the AI endorsement is available only in the Startup Program or also for non-funded tech companies
How Embroker's AI endorsement terms compare to Vouch's AI endorsement terms (limits, exclusions, covered scenarios)
notes: Most frequently mentioned competitor in Gong data — 62 mentions across 1,772 calls (3.5%). Dominant pattern is 'bought at founding, now outgrowing it.' SaaS (24 mentions) and AI (16) are the hottest battlegrounds. 28 of 62 Gong mentions tagged as broker transition scenarios. Recently expanding aggressively into professional services verticals (law, accounting, real estate, consulting) via Everspan partnership — worth monitoring for overlap with Vouch's pro services push. KEY CHANGE (Aug 2025): Embroker launched an Artificial Intelligence Coverage Endorsement auto-included on every eligible Tech E&O / Cyber quote. Covers AI professional services, discrimination/bias, regulatory defense, algorithm removal expenses (up to $150K), at full policy limits with no sublimits. This directly closes the 'no AI endorsements' weakness Vouch has historically exploited. Advisors and content team must stop using AI endorsements as a differentiator vs. Embroker and pivot to advisory depth as the angle.

name: Founder Shield
funding_scale: Acquired by The Baldwin Group (via Millennial Specialty Insurance) in Aug 2021 for ~$9.8M annual revenue at time of acquisition
distribution_notes: Direct-to-buyer digital platform with advisor support. VC partner program serves as an embedded referral channel — offers complimentary coverage gap analyses for portfolio companies. Baldwin Group parent provides access to broader carrier relationships and resources. Claims 25%+ of venture-backed ecosystem as clients.
primary_positioning: Risk management partner for high-growth, venture-backed companies — 'from seed to IPO.' Emphasizes decade-plus startup expertise.
key_differentiators: Deep crypto/web3/DAO vertical expertise
VC partner program (embedded referral pipeline through portfolio company coverage reviews)
20+ lines from a single application
Collaborative real-time platform with peer benchmarking
Baldwin Group scale and carrier relationships behind it
Longest track record in startup insurance space (founded 2012)
pricing_model: Not publicly listed. Fee structure unclear — requires verification.
strengths_vs_vouch: Longer track record in startup space (2012 vs 2019)
Deep crypto/web3/DAO vertical expertise
VC partner program creates embedded referral pipeline
Baldwin Group backing provides scale, resources, and carrier clout
Wider niche coverage (IP, media, fiduciary, SPACs)
Claims 25% of venture-backed ecosystem as clients
weaknesses_vs_vouch: No AI-specific endorsements (bias, regulatory, LLM risk)
Gong data shows prospects report passive service — annual-only touchpoints, reactive rather than proactive
No proprietary underwriting capability (pure broker vs. Vouch's Corex/Hiscox + brokerage model)
Inconsistent customer service per reviews — some report multiple emails and calls to get responses
Less transparent coverage comparison process
Smaller team relative to client base
where_we_win: Vouch wins head-to-head comparisons on advisory depth, proactive gap analysis, AI-specific endorsements, and willingness to shop more carriers. Prospects who have experienced passive annual-only service from FS respond well to Vouch's proactive model. Vouch's proprietary underwriting (Corex/Hiscox) plus full market access gives a structural advantage FS can't match as a pure broker.
where_we_lose: FS has a stronger VC referral pipeline through their partner program — prospects who come in warm through a VC recommendation are harder to displace. FS is also stronger in pure crypto/blockchain verticals where they have deeper niche expertise and carrier relationships. Their Baldwin Group backing gives them credibility with larger, more established companies.
objection_handling: 'Our VC recommended Founder Shield' → Acknowledge FS is a solid shop — then differentiate on AI endorsements, proactive advisory, and proprietary underwriting capability. Offer a no-cost coverage analysis to compare side-by-side. The VC referral gets them in the door; the coverage comparison keeps them.

'Founder Shield has been around longer' → Longevity doesn't equal depth. Vouch has proprietary underwriting capability (not just a broker) and AI-specific coverage that FS doesn't offer. The question is who understands your specific risks better today.

'They cover crypto better' → Fair — crypto/web3 is their strongest vertical. But ask whether their carrier access has kept up. Vouch's portfolio of crypto clients and direct MGA capacity for token and smart contract coverage may offer better terms even in that space.
content_handling: Don't name. Lead with Vouch's advisory relationship and proactive service model vs. passive annual-only broker experiences. For VC-audience content, emphasize Vouch's 175+ VC client portfolio and proactive portfolio company coverage reviews. For AI content, lean into AI endorsements as a differentiator. For crypto content, compete on carrier access and coverage sophistication rather than niche familiarity.
gaps: Fee structure — whether they charge separate broker fees or embed commission in premium is unverified
Specific carrier partnerships
Actual VC partner list and scale of that program
Baldwin Group integration details and how it affects day-to-day operations
Revenue or premium volume (post-acquisition figures not public)
notes: 18 mentions across 1,772 Gong calls (1%). Different competitive dynamic than Embroker — FS shows up primarily as a parallel competitor in active shopping scenarios rather than an incumbent being outgrown. Verticals skew toward crypto/web3 (4), VC/financial services (4), AI (3), SaaS (3). Vouch advisors have redirected at least one prospect toward FS (IT consulting firm), suggesting teams see clear lane separation. FS's VC partner program is their most effective acquisition channel and the hardest to compete against directly.

name: Hiscox
funding_scale: Public (LSE: HSX) — $4.98B global ICWP (FY 2025), $732.7M profit before tax (record). Q1 2026: $1,717.1M group ICWP (+10.2% YoY). $300M share buyback in progress.

target_company_size.revenue_range: Sub-$10M (core DPD); expanding into mid-market via US broker channel and Vouch/Corix acquisition
target_company_size.employee_range: 1-50 (core direct); expanding upmarket through broker channel

distribution_notes: Two US channels: Direct & Partnerships Division (DPD, $171M Q1 2026 ICWP, +9.6% YoY) — self-serve online quoting + digital partnerships (Insureon, TechInsurance, ProWriters); US Broker ($103.7M Q1 2026 ICWP, +6.7% YoY, accelerating from 0.7% in FY2025) — traditional broker-mediated, expanding into life sciences and tech startups via Vouch/Corix technology platform. 23 new DPD partners added in 2025. March 2026 was highest-ever monthly DPD premium. Also has multi-year distribution deal with Vouch post-acquisition. AI voice agent deployed in US contact center for purchasing and claims FNOL, delivering 30% reduction in interactions requiring an advisor.
primary_positioning: Digital-first small business specialty insurer — online quoting in minutes, 600K+ US customers, 180+ professions covered. Backed by Lloyd's heritage and AM Best A financial strength.
key_differentiators: Self-serve online quoting and binding in minutes across 180+ professions
600K+ US customers — largest digital direct small-business insurer
Standalone cyber coverage sold direct online (from $30/month)
Lloyd's heritage (est. 1901) and AM Best A financial strength
Annual thought leadership reports (Cyber Readiness, Underinsurance)
AI-powered operational capabilities: AI voice agent in contact center, AI-enhanced lead underwriting in London Market, automated triage and AI-powered coverage assessments in claims
Vouch/Corix acquisition gives access to startup and life sciences distribution plus cutting-edge broker technology
pricing_model: ~$109/month average ($1,305/year). BOP ~$169/month. Cyber from $30/month. WC from $72/month. 5% multi-product discount. Significantly cheaper than Vouch for simple small-business policies. Digital-direct pricing advantage from underwriting automation. Source: hiscox.com and third-party review sites (US News, NerdWallet, MoneyGeek).
strengths_vs_vouch: Self-serve speed — online quoting and binding in minutes, no advisor needed for simple risks
Scale — 600K+ US customers, $4.98B global premium, $274.7M US Q1 2026 ICWP
Lower price point (~$109/month average) for simple small-business coverage
Carrier-level underwriting control — can innovate on products and pricing faster than a broker
Breadth of profession coverage (180+ professions) far exceeding Vouch's vertical focus
Standalone cyber sold direct online — few carriers offer this
AI operational deployment ahead of most insurers: voice agent handling purchasing/FNOL, AI-powered claims triage, AI lead underwriting
Vouch/Corix acquisition gives them startup/life sciences distribution and broker technology to close the advisory gap over time
weaknesses_vs_vouch: No advisory depth — self-serve model means coverage gaps go unidentified until a claim. Gong data consistently shows companies outgrowing Hiscox without realizing it (Entry 7: SaaS client was 'significantly underinsured' after growth; Entry 14: AI client had only $250K cyber when contract required $10M).

Claims handling reputation — Trustpilot 1.6/5 (944 reviews). Common complaints: weeks/months waiting for claims specialists, denied claims for incidents believed covered, every excuse used to avoid payout. BBB complaints corroborate pattern.
No customer self-service portal for existing policies — every mid-term change requires phone/email, creating friction for companies used to managing things digitally
Renewal pricing opacity — 30-250% premium hikes reported with no explanation (BBB, Trustpilot). One 2026 complaint described 250% increase after reclassification without customer conversation.

Single-carrier paper — Vouch shops 500+ carriers for best terms. Hiscox can only offer its own products. When prospects need specialized coverage (crypto crime, high-limit cyber, complex E&O), Hiscox either declines or offers inadequate sublimits.
No AI-specific or startup-specific endorsements — no coverage for AI bias, regulatory defense, or LLM-specific risk. No milestone-driven reviews tied to fundraising, hiring, or product launches.

Rejects specialized risks that Vouch can place through market access — Gong Entry 13: medical device firm rejected by Hiscox, Vouch placed through legacy market. Entry 1: crypto-adjacent accounting firm couldn't get adequate E&O through standard digital platforms.
D&O not fully self-serve (placed through CyberPolicy partnership), limiting the 'buy everything online' value prop
No startup empathy or ecosystem affiliation — no YC backing, no VC network, no fundraise-triggered coverage reviews
where_we_win: Vouch wins when the prospect has outgrown Hiscox's self-serve model — the most common trigger in Gong data. Key inflection points: (1) Fundraise requiring D&O with investor-grade terms (Entry 2: AI startup needed D&O before month-end for Series A, Vouch bound same day). (2) Enterprise contract demanding higher limits (Entry 14: AI client needed to jump from $250K to $10M cyber — Hiscox maxed out, Vouch sourced competitive quotes from partner carriers). (3) Bad service experience (Entry 19: prospect frustrated with broker unresponsiveness and turnover, cited Vouch platform advantages like self-service certificates). (4) Coverage complexity beyond simple GL/E&O (Entry 5: crypto company needed modular carrier selection across D&O, E&O, cyber, crime — Vouch could mix and match carriers, Hiscox offered only standard packages). (5) Broker transition from 'set it and forget it' (13 of 38 Gong mentions tagged Broker Transition). Vouch's advisory depth, proactive gap analysis, full market access, and ability to place specialized risks through 500+ carriers are structural advantages a single-carrier direct platform cannot replicate.
where_we_lose: Vouch loses when the prospect is cost-driven and simple — freelancers, solopreneurs, or early-stage companies buying basic GL + E&O who just want to check the insurance box. Hiscox's $109/month average and 5-minute online quote win these buyers decisively. Inertia is powerful: with 600K+ customers, most never revisit coverage until something forces them to (contract requirement, claim, fundraise). Hiscox's Vouch/Corix acquisition also creates a new dynamic: as they integrate the broker technology and expand into startups and life sciences, the advisory gap may narrow over time. Worth monitoring whether the acquisition translates into a real product and distribution improvement or remains a technology bolt-on.
objection_handling: 'Hiscox is cheaper' → For basic coverage, yes. Run a coverage comparison on what's actually covered. Gong data shows companies that bought Hiscox at formation are consistently underinsured when they come to Vouch — sublimits too low for actual exposure, no coverage for AI-specific or crypto-specific risks, and limits that haven't kept pace with growth. Total cost of risk (premium + gap exposure) matters more than premium alone.

'We already have Hiscox' → When did you last review coverage? Hiscox doesn't do proactive reviews. Ask: have you raised funding, signed enterprise contracts, hired significantly, or expanded product lines since you bought that policy? In Gong data, 13 of 38 Hiscox mentions are Broker Transition scenarios — companies that bought at formation and never revisited. One SaaS client had a bare-bones professional liability with only $200K IP infringement sublimit while their business had grown significantly.

'Hiscox is easier — quote in 5 minutes' → Speed matters for simple risks. But the 5-minute quote doesn't assess whether you need AI-specific endorsements, whether your D&O limits match your cap table, or whether your cyber coverage addresses your actual PII exposure. An AI client came to Vouch because they had $250K cyber from Hiscox and a contract requiring $10M — the 5-minute quote got them insured, but it didn't get them covered.

'Hiscox is big and established — I trust them' → Hiscox is a strong carrier — we actually place policies through Hiscox-owned entities. The question is whether you want a single carrier's standard products or a broker who can shop the full market for your specific situation. When a medical device engineering firm was rejected by Hiscox because they didn't fit standard categories, Vouch placed them through Chubb, CNA, CFC, and Beazley. Market access matters when your risk isn't simple.

'Hiscox has good cyber coverage' → They do sell standalone cyber direct online, which is rare. But their cyber sublimits for small business policies are often critically low. One prospect had $10K sublimits in areas where typical incidents cost $50K-$300K. And as companies scale, Hiscox's cyber limits max out well below what enterprise contracts require — Vouch can layer primary and excess coverage across multiple carriers to hit $5M, $10M, or higher.
content_handling: Don't name. Lead with Vouch's advisory depth vs. self-serve limitations, milestone-driven coverage reviews, full market access (500+ carriers), and ability to place specialized or complex risks that single-carrier platforms decline or underinsure.
gaps: Specific policy form language and exclusions for Hiscox small business products
Customer retention and churn rates
Detailed pricing by coverage line and company size beyond averages
Vouch/Corix acquisition integration timeline and regulatory approval status
Mary Boyd's specific strategic priorities for US business beyond public statements
Whether Hiscox plans startup-specific or AI-specific products leveraging Vouch/Corix capabilities
How the AI voice agent and automated claims triage perform in practice — customer satisfaction data beyond the 30% reduction claim
Hiscox renewal pricing practices — how systematic are the 30-250% hikes vs. isolated incidents
notes: 2nd most mentioned competitor in Gong data — 38 mentions across call database (behind Embroker at 62). Vertical breakdown: SaaS (16), AI (9), IT Consulting (3), HealthTech (3), Hardware (3), Crypto (2), Accounting & Finance (1), Fintech (1). Stage breakdown: Prospect (18), Client (11), Renewal (9). 13 of 38 tagged Broker Transition — Vouch frequently wins business away from Hiscox. 13 also tagged Pricing, confirming cost is the primary Hiscox advantage. Unique dual dynamic: Hiscox is simultaneously competitor (direct channel) and carrier partner (Corix/Vouch acquisition). The acquisition (announced Aug 2025) gives Hiscox startup/life sciences distribution and cutting-edge broker technology to accelerate US broker platform digitization. Vouch remains independent broker with multi-year distribution deal. Hiscox USA showing accelerating growth: Q1 2026 ICWP $274.7M (+8.5%), with US Broker accelerating to 6.7% from 0.7% in FY2025 — broker channel recovery driven by streamlined workflows and expansion into life sciences and tech startups (likely Vouch/Corix influence). Hiscox deploying AI aggressively on operations side: AI voice agent for purchasing/FNOL (30% reduction in advisor-required interactions), AI-powered claims triage, AI lead underwriting in London Market. Not yet deploying AI in coverage products (no AI endorsements). Mary Boyd appointed USA CEO in mid-2024, replacing Kevin Kerridge. Change programme targeting $75M P&L benefit in 2026, $200M annual by 2028.

name: Coalition
funding_scale: $800M raised (Series F, $30M from Mitsui Sumitomo, Mar 2025); valuation ~$5B

target_company_size.revenue_range: SMB through mid-market — up to $1B for standard products; up to $5B for Active Cyber Policy

distribution_notes: Primarily broker-mediated — brokers access coverage via Coalition's broker platform and co-branded partner portal. APIs available for digital distribution partners. Does not sell direct to buyers at scale. 130,000+ customers across US and Canada. Allianz partnership (May 2026) will extend distribution globally, with Coalition taking over Allianz's standalone commercial cyber portfolio.
primary_positioning: 'Active Insurance' — the world's first insurance designed to actively prevent digital risk before it strikes, combining broad coverage with embedded cybersecurity tools (monitoring, threat detection, incident response).
key_differentiators: Active Insurance model — coverage bundled with real-time security monitoring and threat detection
Proprietary cyber risk scanning at quote — instant technical risk assessment without lengthy questionnaires
Vanishing Retention — retentions decrease year-over-year for claim-free policyholders
Published pricing transparency — openly explains how cyber pricing works
Own licensed carrier (Coalition Insurance Company, AM Best A-) plus capacity from Arch, Allianz, Swiss Re, Lloyd's, Ascot
Allianz global exclusive partnership (2026) — positions Coalition to become largest cyber insurer globally
Annual Cyber Claims Report — high-authority thought leadership used to educate brokers and buyers
pricing_model: Risk-based pricing using automated technical scanning at application. Transparent about methodology. Generally competitive vs. traditional markets. Active Cyber Policy launched April 2025 — available for companies up to $5B revenue, up to $15M limits.
strengths_vs_vouch: Cyber-only depth — Coalition's entire operation is purpose-built for cyber risk; Vouch is a generalist startup broker
Own carrier paper (Coalition Insurance Company, AM Best A-) — underwriting control and pricing flexibility Vouch doesn't have
Integrated security tooling — real-time monitoring, MDR, incident response included; Vouch offers coverage only
Scale — 130,000+ customers, Allianz partnership makes them the de facto global cyber platform
Broker channel dominance — strong broker relationships, purpose-built platform; many brokers default to Coalition for cyber
Vanishing Retention is a compelling policyholder loyalty hook
Deep claims data (2024 Cyber Claims Report) — used to educate market and validate underwriting decisions
Active Cyber Policy available up to $5B revenue — serves companies far larger than Vouch's core ICP
weaknesses_vs_vouch: Cyber-only (effectively) — Coalition's D&O and executive risk lines are secondary; Vouch packages cyber with D&O, E&O, GL, property, crime as a full startup insurance broker
No startup-specific advisory model — Coalition is broker-mediated but not startup-milestone-oriented; no fundraise-triggered reviews, no YC affiliation
Broker distance from buyer — brokers place Coalition; the buyer has no direct advisor relationship with Coalition, only with their broker
No AI-specific endorsements tailored to startup AI risk (bias, regulatory, LLM exposure) — Coalition covers AI cyber threats but not AI product liability the way Vouch does
Not a full-service broker — can't replace Vouch for companies that need a single broker managing all lines
where_we_win: Vouch wins when the prospect needs more than cyber — a growth-stage startup buying D&O for a fundraise, E&O for a contract requirement, GL for a lease, all from one broker who understands their stage. Coalition doesn't play in that multi-line advisory role. Vouch also wins on startup-specific expertise: milestone-driven reviews, VC alignment, fundraising as a trigger, AI-specific product liability endorsements.
where_we_lose: Vouch loses when the prospect only needs standalone cyber and their broker already has a Coalition relationship. Coalition's broker platform is frictionless, and the Active Insurance model (monitoring + coverage bundled) is a compelling standalone cyber story. If the buyer is cost-shopping cyber only, Coalition is often the benchmark.
objection_handling: 'Our broker placed us with Coalition for cyber' → Coalition is a strong cyber-only carrier. The question is whether cyber alone covers your risk. If you've raised a round, taken on enterprise contracts, or have employees, you likely need D&O, E&O, and more. Coalition can't provide that through a single advisor.

'Coalition monitors our security — isn't that enough?' → Monitoring detects and reports risk; it doesn't cover the gaps in your business insurance stack. Ask: who reviews your D&O limits when you close a round? Who helps you meet enterprise contract insurance requirements? Coalition's platform isn't built to answer those questions.

'Coalition's Vanishing Retention is a great feature' → It's a smart retention hook on the cyber side. But coverage that doesn't evolve as you scale — new hires, new products, new investors — creates gaps that no deductible discount can offset.
content_handling: Don't name. Lead with Vouch's full-service advisory model vs. single-line cyber-only carriers. Counter Coalition's 'Active Insurance' narrative by emphasizing that monitoring isn't advising — a broker who understands your business at every stage is a different value proposition than a tech platform that detects threats. For AI vertical content, lean into AI-specific product liability endorsements as differentiation (Coalition covers AI-related cyber threats, not AI product risk).
gaps: No confirmed Gong mentions — Coalition not yet appearing in Vouch call data as of 2026-05-21 (first mention reported May 2026 prospect call)
Specific policy form exclusions and coverage terms vs. Vouch comparable policies
Coalition's D&O product depth and carrier capacity — unclear how competitive vs. dedicated D&O market
Broker compensation structure (commission rates, contingencies)
Revenue or GWP figures — not publicly disclosed
Whether Allianz transition affects US SMB product or just large commercial
notes: Coalition is the largest cyber MGA/insurtech by policy count in North America. Their Allianz exclusive global partnership (announced May 6, 2026) is the biggest recent move — positions them to absorb Allianz's commercial cyber portfolio across US, UK, Australia, Germany, Denmark, Sweden and beyond. This is a step-change in scale. Acquired Wirespeed (Nov 2025) for enhanced cyber risk prevention capability. First prospect mention logged May 2026 — expect Coalition to show up more frequently as they grow broker channel and Allianz deal closes. Primary competitive threat is on standalone cyber, not full-line startup insurance. Watch for any move into startup D&O or multi-line programs.
